title: The Feast of the Most Holy Trinity
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## June 7, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Trinity Sunday, the first Sunday after Pentecost, marking the conclusion of the Easter season and transition into Ordinary Time. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date were likely Proverbs 8:1-4, 22-31 (Wisdom's role in creation), Psalm 8, Romans 5:1-5 (peace and hope through the Spirit), and John 16:12-15 (Jesus on the role of the Holy Spirit). Trinity Sunday emphasizes the doctrine of the Trinity and God's threefold nature, traditionally a theologically dense observance in Anglican worship.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was actively grappling with its response to the national racial justice uprising that had intensified following George Floyd's murder on May 25. The presiding bishop and church leadership had issued statements supporting the Black Lives Matter movement and calling for institutional examination of racism within the church itself. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kee Sloan (elected in 2018), was engaged in its own discernment around these issues, as many Episcopal congregations nationwide were organizing anti-racism working groups and making public commitments to racial justice.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the midst of historic protests following George Floyd's death, with large demonstrations occurring downtown throughout the first week of June. The Denver Police Department faced significant scrutiny following reports of aggressive responses to peaceful protestors, including the use of pepper spray and projectiles during demonstrations on May 28-30. City officials and community leaders were in active dialogue about police reform, with Mayor Michael Hancock calling for investigations into police conduct. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, historically a center of activism and home to diverse communities, was a focal point for these demonstrations.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

George Floyd's death in Minneapolis police custody on May 25 had sparked the largest sustained protest movement in decades, with demonstrations occurring in all 50 states by June 7. President Trump faced widespread criticism for his response, including his photo op at St. John's Church in Washington D.C. on June 1 (which involved clearing peaceful protestors with tear gas), creating significant backlash within religious communities including the Episcopal Church. The nation was simultaneously contending with the COVID-19 pandemic (over 110,000 deaths by this date) and questions about reopening religious gatherings, creating complex pastoral challenges for church leaders.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Kingdom was experiencing civil unrest with statue-removal movements gaining momentum, as George Floyd protests reverberated globally and raised questions about colonial monuments and systemic racism. Brazil was becoming a global epicenter of COVID-19 cases under President Jair Bolsonaro's dismissive approach to the pandemic. Hong Kong was preparing to vote on a controversial national security law that would fundamentally alter the autonomous region's political freedoms.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The nation was experiencing profound moral and spiritual reckoning around systemic racism, with urgent questions about the role of institutions—including religious institutions—in perpetuating or challenging racial injustice. Many churches were facing their congregants' expectation that they take explicit stands on the Black Lives Matter movement, creating theological and pastoral tension in some congregations. The mood was one of simultaneous crisis (pandemic, police violence, economic uncertainty) and possibility (widespread mobilization for change, intergenerational activism), though tempered by fear and exhaustion from ongoing COVID-19 restrictions on in-person gathering.
